content.problemStatement:
You are given a complete graph†^{\dagger}† on NNN vertices with the edges having weights either 000 or 111.

Unfortunately you have forgotten which edges had weights of 111 and which had weights of 000. But you do remember that there were exactly MMM edges with weight of 111.

Consider the Minimum Spanning Tree Problem on this complete graph. Let WWW denote the sum of the weights of edges that are used in the minimum spanning tree.

Find the sum of all possible distinct WWW for complete graphs having exactly MMM weight 111 edges.

†^{\dagger}† A complete graph on NNN vertices has N⋅(N−1)2\frac{N \cdot (N - 1)}{2}2N⋅(N−1)​ edge with an edge (X,Y)(X, Y)(X,Y) for all 1≤X<Y≤N1 \le X < Y \le N1≤X<Y≤N.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains NNN and MMM - the number of nodes, and the number of weight 111 edges.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the sum off all possible values of WWW.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1 : The only possible value of WWW is 111. For example, one graph that obtains it is having edges (1,2)(1, 2)(1,2) and (1,3)(1, 3)(1,3) as weight 111, with the edge (2,3)(2, 3)(2,3) being weight 000.
Then, one possible MST contains the edges (1,2)(1, 2)(1,2) and (2,3)(2, 3)(2,3), having a total weight of 111.
Test Case 2 : All edges are weight 111. So any MST will be of weight 222.
Test Case 3 : It is possible to obtain graphs with MST weight being 000 or 111.
Test Case 4 : It is possible to obtain graphs with MST weight being 222 or 333.
